preamble: Lateral palatalization involves the shift from the alveolar lateral [l] to the palatal lateral [ʎ]. This change is extremely common when /l/ is followed by the palatal glide [j] or high front vowels. It is a defining historical development in Italian, Spanish, and several Slavic languages. The process often results in the loss of alveolar contact in favor of a dorsal contact against the hard palate.

examples.note: Spanish historically went through a [ʎ] stage before shifting to [x].
The palatal lateral /ʎ/ (written 'lh') is a core phoneme in Portuguese.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
